retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Proteins Enzymes -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: There are six main categories of enzymes: oxidoreductases, transferases, hydrolases, lyases, isomerases, and ligases. Each category carries out a general type of reaction but catalyzes many different specific reactions within their own category. Some enzymes, called apoenzymes, are inactive until they are bound to a cofactor, which activates the enzyme. A cofactor can be either metal ions (e.g., Zn) or organic compounds that attach, either covalently or noncovalently, to the enzyme. The cofactor and apoenzyme complex is called a holoenzyme. Enzymes are proteins comprised of amino acids linked together in one or more polypeptide chains. This sequence of amino acids in a polypeptide chain is called the primary structure. This, in turn, determines the three-dimensional structure of the enzyme, including the shape of the active site. The secondary structure of a protein describes the localized polypeptide chain structures, e.g., α-helices or β-sheets.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Cytochrome P450 -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: CYP enzymes consist of an active site made of a heme-iron center. The iron compound is bound to the protein by a cysteine thiolate molecule. Substrate(s) bind to the active site at the heme group, which induces a conformational change of the enzyme's active site. Reductases are then responsible for electron transfer from NAD(P)H. Oxygen binds to the ferrous-heme group following the reduction of iron. Another electron is added via reduction, creating a Fe-O2 group known as a peroxide state. The peroxide group is short-lived as it gets protonated twice to release water and another compound known as P450 Compound 1 (FeO3+). This activity allows for the acquisition of the necessary and further hydroxylated to a more excretable, hydrophilic compound. In general, the result of the mechanism appears as such: O2 + NAD(P)H + H+ + RH → NAD(P)+ + H2O + ROH

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Cytochrome P450 -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: It is crucial to keep in mind that many drugs that serve as either an inducer or inhibitor of an isozyme also act as a substrate. For the sake of eliminating redundancy, they were not mentioned twice here. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Cytochrome P450 -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: CYP enzymes undergo a multitude of reactions, each with slightly different variations depending on the enzyme and substrate involved. As monooxygenases, CYP's primary role is the addition of an oxygen atom to the substrate. In general, the mechanism CYP exhibits in catalyzing reactions depends on a few simple steps.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Proteins Enzymes -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Catalysis by approximation happens when two different substrates work together in the active site to form the enzyme-substrate complex. A common example of this involves water entering the active site to donate or receive a proton after a substrate has already bound to form better nucleophiles that can form and break bonds easier.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Fatty Acid Oxidation -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Step 1. The first enzyme required is called acyl CoA dehydrogenase, and like other enzymes involved in the handling of fatty acids, it is specific to chain length. Members of this enzyme family include long-chain, medium-chain, and short-chain acyl CoA dehydrogenases (LCAD), (MCAD), and (SCAD), respectively. These enzymes catalyze the formation of a trans double bond between the alpha and beta carbons on acyl CoA molecules by removing two electrons to produce one molecule of FAD(H2), which eventually accounts for 1.5 ATP molecules produced in the electron transport chain (ETC).

retrieved_docs.title: Myeloperoxidase Deficiency -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The majority of patients with MPO deficiency are asymptomatic with no increase in infections. However recurrent severe infections with Candida Albicans have been observed only in patients who also suffered from other conditions such as diabetes mellitus. Thus, in these patients, it is not clear if the infections were a mere outcome of MPO deficiency or if other MPO independent mechanisms were also responsible. In a study conducted by European researchers, only 50% of the complete MPO deficient patients had infectious complications, the rest were asymptomatic, and only 10% of the patients suffered from life-threatening infectious complications.  The lack of serious infections in MPO deficiency compared to CGD emphasizes the importance of the reactive oxygen species such as amplified hydrogen peroxide generation serving as a potent bactericidal agent that protects patients with MPO deficiency. However, the absence of MPO-mediated species such as HOCl appears to be crucial in the failure to abort fungal infections such as Candidiasis.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Proteins Enzymes -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Metal ion catalysis involves the participation of a metal ion at the active site of the enzyme, which can help make the attacking residue a better nucleophile and stabilize any negative charge in the active site.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Cytochrome P450 -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Research in the molecular field of CYP enzymes is still developing, and the addition of the human genome project will only prove more vital as an element in personalized medicine. Still, it is a known fact that polymorphism plays a significant role in drug metabolism due to the inherited CYP activity of individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Heme Synthesis -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Heme has a variety of functions. As a cofactor, it allows for the following [1] : Oxygen transport in hemoglobin Storage in myoglobin A prosthetic group for cytochrome p450 enzymes A reservoir of iron Electron shuttle of enzymes in the electron transport chain Cellular respiration Signal transduction-heme regulates the antioxidant response to circadian rhythms, microRNA processing Cellular differentiation and proliferation

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Proteins Enzymes -- Molecular Level
retrieved_docs.content: General acid-base catalysis takes place when a molecule other than water acts as a proton donor or acceptor. Water can be one of the proton donors or acceptors in the reaction, but it cannot be the only one. This characteristic can sometimes help make catalytic residues better nucleophiles, so they will more easily attack substrate amino acids.

retrieved_docs.title: Medium-Chain Acyl-CoA Dehydrogenase Deficiency -- Pathophysiology -- Causes of Phenotypic Variation
retrieved_docs.content: MCADD is considered a "conformational disorder;" however, clinical manifestations of the disease span a broad spectrum of severity, even for those patients with the same genotype. The pathogenic phenotype of the disease appears dependent on additional extrinsic and intrinsic factors that are not clearly understood. [4] One extrinsic factor to consider is riboflavin. As a flavoenzyme, MCADD depends on adequate riboflavin derivatives for its function. A clinical trial testing MCAD activity in the lymphocytes of patients with MCADD reported increased enzyme activity after riboflavin supplementation. However, the genetic profile of the test individuals was not reported in this study. [16] A second study examined lipid and protein oxidative damage in patients with MCADD who were supplemented with carnitine or carnitine and riboflavin. They were compared to individuals who did not receive any supplementation and healthy controls. The study reported increased protein oxidative damage and altered antioxidant defense in unsupplemented and carnitine-supplemented patients. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Myoglobin -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Myoglobin is a protein located primarily in the striated muscles of vertebrates. MB is the gene encoding myoglobin in humans. It encodes a single polypeptide chain with one oxygen binding site. Myoglobin contains a heme prosthetic group that can reversibly bind to oxygen. The body uses it as an oxygen storage protein in muscle. It is able to bind and release oxygen depending on the oxygen concentration in the cell. Its primary function, as a result, is to supply oxygen to myocytes. Myoglobin also functions in the hemostasis of nitric oxide. It additionally plays a role in the detoxification of reactive oxygen species. Myoglobin is the reason for the red color of the muscle of most vertebrates. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Essential Amino Acids -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Besides 20 amino acids that participate in protein synthesis, recently, 2 more new amino acids have been described—selenocysteine and pyrrolysine. Selenocysteine occurs at the active site of several enzymes, including thioredoxin reductase and glutathione peroxidase. Pyrrolysine is not present in humans but is used in the biosynthesis of proteins in some methanogenic species, such as archaea and bacteria. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Superoxides -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Currently, research has shown that the superoxide molecule and its progeny are present in most aerobic cells and that they portray an immense array of important functions in a variety of cell types. [4] [12] [14] [15] Superoxide production occurs in cells through spontaneous processes as well as enzymatic oxidation. [16] Given its ubiquitous presence within the cells of the human body, the conclusion is that the presence of superoxide also accompanies the presence of SODs, the main defender against the potentially damaging reactions of these molecules. [5] The production of superoxides is achievable by redox reactions with molecular oxygen by the following substances: thiols, flavins, quinones, catecholamines, and pterins. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Aflatoxin Toxicity -- Toxicokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: The most common route of entry of aflatoxin into the human body is ingestion. After entry, it is metabolized by microsomal mixed-function oxidase (MFO) enzymes in the liver to reactive epoxide intermediate. MFO is an enzyme of the CYP450 superfamily. This epoxide intermediate (8,9-epoxide) is responsible for DNA mutation. The predominant mutation has been identified as the G→T transversion in codon 249 of the p53 tumor suppressor gene. This epoxide can bind to other macromolecules like RNA and proteins, causing cellular dysregulation. It is also responsible for inhibiting proteins, RNA, and DNA synthesis. Another pathway to toxicity is glutathione depletion and subsequent toxicity from reactive oxygen species. Other pathways of carcinogenesis, catalyzed by prostaglandin H (PGH) synthase and lipid peroxidase (LPO), are under study.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 566
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymes are biological catalysts that are organized into several major subclasses, including isomerases, ligases, lyases, oxidoreductases, and transferases. Isomerases catalyze intramolecular rearrangements of chemical structures, ligases join two substrates together with the concomitant hydrolysis of a nucleoside triphosphate, lyases cleave bonds by means other than hydrolysis or oxidation, oxidoreductases mediate oxidation‑reduction reactions, and transferases move functional groups from one molecule to another. Beyond their essential metabolic functions, enzymes also play a central role in pharmacological effects, serving as the molecular mechanisms through which many drugs act, modulate physiological pathways, and influence therapeutic outcomes. Recognizing the classification of enzymes and their involvement in drug mechanisms is crucial for effective drug development and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2291
retrieved_docs.content: Chymotrypsin is classified as an enzyme, belonging to the broader enzyme family (enzymy). Within this family, enzymes are organized into major functional subclasses, each catalyzing distinct types of biochemical reactions. The enzyme class includes isomerases (isomerasy), which facilitate structural rearrangements of molecules; ligases (ligasy), which join two molecules together with the concomitant hydrolysis of a diphosphate bond; lyases (lyasy), which cleave bonds by means other than hydrolysis or oxidation, often forming a new double bond or a new ring structure; oxidoreductases (oxidoreduktasy), which mediate oxidation‑reduction reactions by transferring electrons between molecules; and transferases (transferasy), which move functional groups from one molecule to another. Understanding that chymotrypsin is part of this hierarchical enzyme system helps contextualize its specific proteolytic activity within the larger spectrum of enzymatic functions relevant to metabolism and therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 577
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymes, peripheral vasodilators are identified as a type of peripheral vasodilator and are situated within a broader enzyme grouping called enzymy. The enzymy category encompasses several major enzyme subclasses, specifically isomerasy, ligasy, lyasy, oxidoreduktasy, and transferasy. These subclasses correspond to the well‑known families of isomerases, ligases, lyases, oxidoreductases, and transferases, each catalyzing distinct biochemical reactions. Certain members of these enzyme families possess peripheral vasodilatory activity, influencing vascular tone in the peripheral circulation. Understanding that enzymes, peripheral vasodilators belong both to the peripheral vasodilator class and to the larger enzymy classification integrates biochemical and physiological perspectives on vascular regulation and supports more comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1330
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymes are biological catalysts that are organized into several major subclasses, each dedicated to a distinct type of chemical transformation. The subclass isomerases (isomerasy) catalyze the rearrangement of atoms within a molecule, converting it into its isomeric form. Ligases (ligasy) facilitate the joining of two molecules with the concomitant hydrolysis of a small pendant group such as ATP, forming new chemical bonds. Lyases (lyasy) break chemical bonds by means other than hydrolysis or oxidation, often generating a new double bond or a new ring structure. Oxidoreductases (oxidoreduktasy) mediate oxidation‑reduction reactions, transferring electrons between substrates. Transferases (transferasy) move functional groups, such as methyl or phosphate groups, from one molecule to another. Together, these enzyme subclasses encompass the primary mechanisms by which cells accelerate and regulate metabolic pathways, underscoring their central role in physiology and therapeutic targeting.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1531
retrieved_docs.content: The human cytochrome P450 3A4 protein metabolizes mikonazol, while amiodaron is also a substrate of the cytochrome P450 3A4 protein, indicating that CYP3A4 is responsible for the clearance of both agents. In addition, both mikonazol and amiodaron act as inhibitors of the enzymes described as inhibitory cytochromu P450 CYP2C9 and inhibitory cytochromu P450 CYP3A, meaning they can suppress the activity of these CYP isoforms. Consequently, the metabolism of other drugs that depend on CYP2C9 or CYP3A may be reduced when patients are treated with mikonazol or amiodaron, highlighting the importance of monitoring for potential drug‑drug interactions in clinical care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2080
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymes, peripheral vasodilators are classified as peripheral vasodilators, indicating that these enzymes function to induce dilation of peripheral blood vessels.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 949
retrieved_docs.content: Human cytochrome P450 3A4 protein metabolizes the antifungal agent mikonazol and the proton‑pump inhibitor omeprazol, and it also metabolizes the antiarrhythmic drug amiodaron. Both mikonazol and amiodaron act as inhibitors of the cytochrome P450 enzymes CYP2C9 and CYP3A, as indicated by their classification under the inhibitory cytochrome P450 CYP2C9 and inhibitory cytochrome P450 CYP3A categories. This dual role—where CYP3A4 facilitates drug metabolism while the same drugs can inhibit other CYP isoforms—highlights important drug‑enzyme interactions that can affect the pharmacokinetics of co‑administered medications and must be considered in patient care to avoid adverse effects or therapeutic failure.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 450
retrieved_docs.content: Enzymy constitute a broad group of biological catalysts that are organized into several major subclasses based on the type of chemical reaction they facilitate. Among these subclasses are hydrolasy, which catalyze hydrolytic cleavage of bonds; isomerasy, which mediate the rearrangement of atoms within a molecule; ligasy, which join two molecules together with the concomitant hydrolysis of a diphosphate bond; lyasy, which break bonds by means other than hydrolysis or oxidation, often forming a new double bond or a new ring structure; oxidoreduktasy, which catalyze oxidation‑reduction (redox) reactions by transferring electrons between substrates; and transferasy, which transfer functional groups from one molecule to another. Recognizing that hydrolasy, isomerasy, ligasy, lyasy, oxidoreduktasy, and transferasy are all child categories of enzymy provides a foundational framework for understanding enzyme function, metabolic pathway organization, and the development of therapeutic agents that target specific enzymatic activities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 487
retrieved_docs.content: Cytochrome P450 3A4 protein (human) is an important drug‑metabolizing enzyme that catalyzes the biotransformation of both mikonazol and omeprazol. Amiodarone is also a substrate of cytochrome P450 3A4, indicating that this enzyme contributes to the clearance of amiodarone in patients. In addition to being metabolized, amiodarone functions as an inhibitor of several cytochrome P450 isoforms, including inhibitory cytochrome P450 CYP2C9 and inhibitory cytochrome P450 CYP3A, as well as broader inhibitory enzymes. The class of inhibitory enzymes encompasses drugs such as omeprazol and racecadotril, suggesting that these agents can suppress enzymatic activity. Racecadotril is linked to thiorfan through a mapping relationship, indicating a structural or functional derivation from thiorfan. Furthermore, inhibitory enzymes are categorized under the parent group “inhibitory protein synthesis,” reflecting that their inhibitory actions may involve modulation of protein production pathways. Together, these relationships highlight a network in which cytochrome P450 3A4 mediates drug metabolism, while amiodarone and certain other agents act as inhibitors of cytochrome P450 isoforms and related enzymatic systems, influencing drug‑drug interactions and therapeutic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2836
retrieved_docs.content: Mikonazol is classified as an inhibitor of the cytochrome P450 CYP3A enzyme, indicating that it functions as an inhibitory agent targeting CYP3A‑mediated drug metabolism.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1387
retrieved_docs.content: Amylasy and chymotrypsin are both classified as enzymes, indicating that they share the parent entity “enzymy.” Within the broader enzyme family, the subclass entities ligasy, oxidoreduktasy, and transferasy are identified as child categories of “enzymy,” representing the major enzyme classes of ligases, oxidoreductases, and transferases. This hierarchical organization highlights how specific proteins such as amylasy and chymotrypsin fit into the larger enzymatic system and how the enzyme family is subdivided into functional groups that catalyze distinct types of biochemical reactions, which is essential for understanding metabolic pathways and guiding therapeutic interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2203
retrieved_docs.content: Cytochrome P450 3A4 protein, human metabolizes mikonazol, indicating that this major hepatic enzyme is responsible for the biotransformation of the antifungal agent. In contrast, inhibitory cytochromu P450 CYP2C9 is associated with mikonazol in an inhibitory capacity, suggesting that this isoform can reduce the metabolism or activity of the drug. The differing roles of CYP3A4 and CYP2C9 in relation to mikonazol highlight the importance of considering specific cytochrome P450 isoforms when predicting drug clearance, potential drug‑drug interactions, and appropriate dosing strategies for patients receiving mikonazol.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1518
retrieved_docs.content: Akarbóza is classified as an enzyme inhibitor, and racecadotril is likewise classified as an enzyme inhibitor, indicating that both akarbóza and racecadotril belong to the same category of inhibitory enzymes. This shared classification highlights their common pharmacological role in modulating enzymatic activity, which is important for understanding their mechanisms of action and potential therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1555
retrieved_docs.content: Human cytochrome P450 3A4 protein metabolizes both miconazole and amiodarone, indicating that these drugs are substrates of this major hepatic enzyme. In addition, both miconazole and amiodarone act as inhibitors of cytochrome P450 enzymes, specifically inhibiting CYP2C9 and the broader CYP3A family, which can lead to drug‑drug interaction potential when co‑administered with other CYP2C9 or CYP3A substrates. Amiodarone also possesses pharmacologic activity characteristic of adrenergic beta‑antagonists; it antagonizes β‑1‑adrenergic receptors, placing its mechanism of action within the class of beta‑blockers. This β‑1 antagonism contributes to amiodarone’s antiarrhythmic effects and aligns it with other agents that block β‑adrenergic signaling. Understanding that miconazole and amiodarone share metabolic pathways (CYP3A4) and inhibitory effects on CYP2C9 and CYP3A, while amiodarone additionally provides β‑1 blockade, is essential for anticipating interactions, optimizing dosing, and ensuring safe patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2389
retrieved_docs.content: Amiodaron is both a substrate and an inhibitor of the cytochrome P450 enzyme system. Specifically, amiodaron is metabolized by the human cytochrome P450 3A4 protein, an enzyme that metabolizes a wide range of chemicals and drugs. At the same time, amiodaron is classified as an inhibitory cytochrome P450 CYP3A agent, meaning it can suppress the activity of CYP3A enzymes. This dual role as a substrate of CYP3A4 and an inhibitor of CYP3A highlights the potential for amiodaron to affect the metabolism of other concurrently administered medications, making awareness of drug‑drug interactions essential in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2093
retrieved_docs.content: Cyklosporin is described as an inhibitor of enzymes, reflecting an inverse is‑a relationship, and this inhibitory enzyme activity is part of a broader relationship that also involves protein C. The connection indicates that the enzymatic inhibition exerted by cyklosporin extends to the regulation of protein C, highlighting the relevance of cyklosporin’s enzyme‑inhibitory properties in the context of protein C function.
